nursing_diagnosis.term: Utpreget dagtretthet sekundært til kronisk alveolær hypoventilasjon.

interventions.term: Iverksette og følge opp hjemmerespiratorbehandling (non-invasiv eller invasiv ventilasjon), med særskilt fokus på etiske overveielser og pasientdialog knyttet til rask sykdomsforverring ved ALS.

goals.term: Oppnå symptomlindring for tretthet og øke pasientens forventede levetid.

quotes: J Rehabil Med 2006; 38: 250254 Correspondence address: Michael Laub, MD, Dept of Respiratory Medicine, University Hospital, SE-221 85, Lund, Sweden. E-mail: ml@laub.dk Submitted April 21, 2005; accepted March 17, 2006 INTRODUCTION Home mechanical ventilation (HMV) in patients with chronic alveolar hypoventilation secondary to neuromuscular diseases (NMD) has become widely acceptable, as the provision of ventilatory support can provide symptomatic relief and increase life expectancy (1). HMV can be applied as non-invasive ventilation (NIV) most often via a facemask, or as invasive ventilation via a tracheo- stomy. Generally, the use of NIV is much more prevalent than invasive ventilation. DISCUSSION In this prospective register-based study, comprising 352 patients with chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure secondary to neurological diseases, we have shown that 24% of the patients started HMV in an acute situation. In the remaining 268 elective patients we found that the most common motive for starting HMV was daytime sleepiness, which was present in 56% of the patients, yet this was not associated with worse blood gases compared with the patients not complaining of daytime sleepiness. In most of the diagnostic groups the vital capacity was reduced to about 40% of the predicted value, whereas the DMD group exhibited a more substantial reduction to 26%. Furthermore, in these children the BE were significantly lower than in the other groups, which might indicate a more sufficient nocturnal ventilation in the SMA group suggesting an earlier recognition of their respiratory problem. We wish to point out that the ALS group fits well into the general pattern, with respect to acute vs elective launching of HMV, as well as to motives and physiological data. We call attention to this since there is a special focus on patients with ALS regarding not only the rapid aggravation in the disease but the ethical aspects on HMV, especially invasive ventilation, as well. Apart from the Duchenne group the mean VC was approximately 40% of the predicted value for all groups.

nursing_diagnosis.term: Risiko for uoppdaget hypoventilasjon og respiratorisk svikt manifestert som tretthet på dagtid.

interventions.term: Utføre objektive lungefunksjonstester og assistere ved måling av vitalkapasitet som en systematisk del av sykepleiekartleggingen når pasienten rapporterer om tretthet.

goals.term: Sikre tidlig påvisning av respirasjonssvikt og rettidig oppstart av ventilasjonsstøtte før vitalkapasiteten faller under 50 prosent av forventet verdi.

quotes: Mean vital capa- city was close to 40% of predicted, but significantly lower in the Duchenne patients (26% of predicted). Conclusion: Daytime sleepiness was the most common clinical symptom motivating home mechanical ventilation in this group of patients with chronic hypercapnic respiratory insufficiency secondary to neuro/myopathies. Respiratory function testing is therefore suggested to be included in the diagnostic work up of daytime sleepiness in these patients. Key words: respiratory failure, non-invasive ventilation, invasive ventilation, symptoms and signs. Apart from the Duchenne group the mean VC was approximately 40% of the predicted value for all groups. This is well in accordance with the general recommendations from a recent consensus group (2) considering HMV in patients with VCB/50% of predicted and more specifically with the conclusion in a large ALS study (18). More severe reductions in VC in patients with DMD before starting HMV has earlier been shown (19, 20), and in the latter study a group of patients with DMD, who were not considered as immediate candidates for HMV, had a VC of 35% of the predicted value.

source_documents.title: SYMPTOMS, CLINICAL AND PHYSIOLOGICAL FINDINGS MOTIVATING HOME MECHANICAL VENTILATION IN PATIENTS WITH NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASES
source_documents.reasoning_trace: Dokumentet beskriver behandling av pasienter med nevromuskulære sykdommer, deriblant ALS, med hjemmerespirator. Teksten identifiserer tretthet på dagtid som det hyppigste symptomet på respiratorisk svikt og hypoventilasjon i denne pasientgruppen. For ALS-pasienter spesielt fremheves et særskilt behov for etiske overveielser ved bruk av invasiv ventilasjon på grunn av sykdommens raske progresjon. Objektiv lungefunksjonstesting anbefales som et sentralt tiltak ved utredning av dagtretthet. Sykepleiediagnoser i uttrekket knyttes til tretthet og uoppdaget respirasjonssvikt, mens tiltakene omfatter klinisk overvåkning, testing og etisk fundert ventilasjonsstøtte med mål om symptomlindring og forlenget levetid.
